meta.manuscript_location: Shrine of the Book, Israel Museum, Jerusalem

preamble.summary: Isaiah 2 in 1QIsaiah-a contains the famous swords-into-plowshares oracle (vv. 2-4), which parallels Micah 4:1-3. The scroll is well preserved through this chapter. Most variants are orthographic, with a few moderate differences in the Day of the LORD catalogue.
preamble.notable_variants: The shared passage with Micah 4:1-3 (Isaiah 2:2-4) is remarkably stable — 1QIsaiah-a agrees closely with the MT here. A few plene spellings and one or two minor word-form differences appear in the Day of the LORD section (vv. 12-21).
preamble.column_reference: Columns II–III of 1QIsaiah-a contain Isaiah chapter 2.

variant_notes: No significant variant. The scroll reads identically to the MT here.
Identical consonantal text. The eschatological vision of the mountain of the LORD's house reads the same in both traditions.
Plene spelling with waw. The verb from the root y-r-h ('teach, instruct') — the same root as torah — reads identically in meaning.
No significant variant. The scroll reads identically to the MT here.

Plene spelling with additional waw. The reference to forbidden divination practices is identical.
Plene spelling with additional waw. Standard Qumran orthography.

verses.variant_rendering: So humanity is brought low, and each person is humbled

variant_notes: The consonantal text is identical. Some scholars note that 1QIsaiah-a's spacing may suggest a different verse division, but the reading is the same.

Plene spelling with waw. The refrain about human pride being humbled is identical in meaning.
